lessons.title: std::format: Type-Safe Formatted Output
lessons.motivation:
Before C++20, string formatting in C++ was an uncomfortable compromise. `printf` is compact but type-unsafe: a mismatch between a format specifier and the actual argument is undefined behaviour that the compiler only occasionally catches via a warning. `std::cout` with `<<` operators is safe but verbose, with no concise syntax for controlling width, alignment, or numeric bases. `std::to_string` offers minimal control. User-defined types cannot be passed to `printf` at all.

`std::format`, introduced in C++20, solves all of these problems: the format string is checked at compile time, the API is type-safe, the format specifier syntax is borrowed from Python (concise and expressive), and user-defined types plug in via `std::formatter<T>` specialisations.
lessons.theory:
`printf` dates back to C and remains a source of bugs to this day: the format string is a plain character array, and the match between specifiers (`%d`, `%f`, `%s`) and actual arguments is not verified by the compiler (only some compilers warn via attributes). Passing an object of a user-defined class to `printf` is impossible. `std::ostream` is safer but formatting a number requires chaining manipulators (`std::setw`, `std::setfill`, `std::hex`). C++20 introduces `<format>` — a library where the template format string is parsed at compile time, arguments are typed, and the result is returned as a `std::string`.

A `std::format` format string contains *replacement fields* enclosed in braces. Plain `{}` outputs arguments in order. `{0}`, `{1}` are positional references (repeatable: `{0} {1} {0}`). After a colon inside `{}` comes a *format specifier*: `{:.2f}` — floating-point with two decimal places, `{:08x}` — hexadecimal zero-padded to eight characters, `{:#010x}` — the same with the `0x` prefix. Alignment is specified via `<` (left), `>` (right), `^` (centre) with an optional fill character: `{:*<10}` pads the field with asterisks on the right. Output types: `b` — binary, `o` — octal, `x`/`X` — hexadecimal, `e`/`E` — scientific notation, `f` — fixed-point.

Beyond `std::format`, which returns a `std::string`, the standard provides functions that write directly to an iterator: `std::format_to(out, fmt, args...)` writes characters to the output iterator `out` (e.g. `std::back_inserter(buf)` or a raw pointer), avoiding an extra allocation. `std::format_to_n(out, n, fmt, args...)` additionally limits the number of characters written. `std::formatted_size(fmt, args...)` returns the size of the result string without writing anything — useful for pre-allocating a buffer.

To support user-defined types, specialise the `std::formatter<T>` template. The specialisation has two methods: `parse(ctx)` parses the user-defined portion of the format specifier (the fragment between `:` and `}`) and stores parameters in the struct's fields; `format(value, ctx)` writes the result to `ctx.out()` and returns the updated iterator. Both methods must be `constexpr`/`const` where appropriate (see the example). For situations where the format string is known only at runtime (e.g. read from a config file), `std::vformat(fmt, std::make_format_args(args...))` is available — compile-time checking is absent in that case.

examples.title: Format string syntax: numbers, alignment, positional arguments
examples.expectedOutput:
int: 42, float: 3.14, string: hello
hex: ff, HEX: FF, oct: 377, bin: 11111111
padded hex: 0x000000ff
float fixed: 3.1416
float sci:   1.234568e+05
sign:  +42, -42
|     right|left      |  center  |
|0000000042|hi********|---mid----|
ping pong ping

examples.title: Custom formatter and format_to
examples.expectedOutput:
default: rgb(255,0,0)
hex:     #ff0000
teal:    #008080
colors: rgb(255,0,0) and #008080
formatted_size of 3.14: 4

lessons.title: std::span: A Safe View Over Contiguous Data
lessons.motivation:
Before C++20, functions that operated on sequences of elements had no good way to accept "any contiguous range". The C-style approach `void process(int* data, size_t n)` is unsafe: the pointer may be null, the size is easy to get wrong, and the compiler guarantees nothing. Taking `const std::vector<int>&` is safer but forces the caller to hold a `vector` — a `std::array`, a C array, or a subrange of a vector cannot be passed without copying. A template parameter `template<typename Container>` removes the container type restriction but loses the contiguousness guarantee and obscures the interface.

`std::span<T>`, introduced in C++20, is the standard solution: a non-owning view over a contiguous sequence of elements. It accepts `std::vector`, `std::array`, C arrays, and any other contiguous range without copying data and without any additional allocations — it is simply a pointer-plus-size pair wrapped in a type-safe interface.
lessons.theory:
Before C++20 there was no single way to write a function that accepted "any contiguous range of integers". A pointer with a size (`int* data, size_t n`) works but is unsafe and does not self-document. `const std::vector<int>&` rejects `std::array` and C arrays. A template parameter accepts anything, losing the contiguousness contract. `std::span<T>` is a non-owning, zero-cost view: it stores a pointer to the start and an element count, allocates no memory, and does not manage the data's lifetime. Any contiguous range — a vector, a C array, a `std::array`, or a subspan of another span — implicitly converts to `std::span<T>`. A `std::span<const T>` parameter makes a function read-only while still accepting all of the above without any changes at the call site.

The core `std::span` interface is straightforward: `s.size()` returns the element count, `s.data()` the raw pointer, `s.size_bytes()` the size in bytes (`size() * sizeof(T)`). Element access is via `s[i]`, `s.front()`, and `s.back()` (bounds checking is not guaranteed by default). A span is a range: it has `begin()`/`end()`, so it works with range-based for loops and standard algorithms. `std::span<T>` has a dynamic extent (the size is stored at runtime), while `std::span<T, N>` has a static extent: the size is fixed at compile time, `size()` is `constexpr`, and the compiler will reject an array of the wrong length.

A span provides three subview operations, all without allocation: `s.first(n)` — the first `n` elements, `s.last(n)` — the last `n`, `s.subspan(offset, count)` — `count` elements starting at `offset` (or `s.subspan(offset)` — to the end). Each returns a new `std::span` pointing into the same memory. This makes spans a natural fit for sliding windows, partitioning data, and divide-and-conquer algorithms without any extra copying.

For working with raw binary data, the standard provides `std::as_bytes(s)` — returning `std::span<const std::byte>` — and `std::as_writable_bytes(s)` — returning `std::span<std::byte>` (only for a non-const span). These are useful for serialisation, network I/O, and binary protocols. The canonical API design rule: **any function that reads a contiguous sequence should take `std::span<const T>`, and any function that modifies it should take `std::span<T>`**. This is the one parameter type that lets callers pass a vector, a C array, a `std::array`, or a subrange — with no overloads and no templates.
